misconceptionSummary: Students swap column names: calling tenths 'tens' and hundredths 'hundreds'.

steps.narration: The four is to the right of the decimal point. That is the tenths column, not the tens column. Tens is to the left — it would make the number much bigger.

visuals.ruleText: Left of the decimal = whole-number columns (tens, hundreds…). Right of the decimal = fraction columns (tenths, hundredths…).
visuals.subText: The 'th' ending signals a fraction column

steps.narration: The 'th' suffix is the clue. Tenths, hundredths, thousandths — all fractions, all to the right. Tens, hundreds, thousands — all whole multiples, all to the left.
Four in the tenths column is worth four tenths — point four. If it were in the tens column it would be worth forty. The decimal point makes all the difference.

visuals.label: Right of the decimal point → fraction column

steps.narration: Zero point four. Tenths, not tens. The position right of the decimal point tells you it is a fraction.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: saying the first digit right of the decimal is in the 'tens' column. That column is 'tenths' — a fraction, not a multiple.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: The 'th' ending is the giveaway. Tenths means divided by ten. Tens means multiplied by ten. They are on opposite sides of the decimal point.
